source: Derived from beneficial-insects.json by inverting preyOn relationships. Notes for the top-priority pests are hand-authored to surface the wait-before-spraying moment; remaining entries carry a templated note that names the categories of natural enemies present.

entries.note: Tracked natural enemies: microbial controls (1). Scout for visible activity before resorting to broad-spectrum sprays.

Wait 3-5 days before spraying. Look for ladybug larvae, lacewing eggs (white stalked threads under leaves), and dark mummified aphids: the parasitoid wasps and predator population is already responding. A soap spray now will reset the predator population back to zero alongside the aphids.

The single most natural-enemy-rich aphid in vegetable gardens. Aphidius colemani, syrphid larvae, ladybug larvae, and lacewing larvae all hit this species hard. Hold off on broad-spectrum sprays; a targeted spot wash with water is enough while predators build up.
Aphidius wasps and hover fly larvae specialize on lettuce aphid. Inspect for parasitoid mummies (swollen tan aphids stuck in place) before spraying.
Tracked natural enemies: parasitoids (1). Scout for visible activity before resorting to broad-spectrum sprays.
Generalist aphid predators (ladybug larvae, lacewing larvae, hover fly larvae) provide the natural-enemy response. Avoid broad-spectrum sprays on asparagus ferns.
Tracked natural enemies: predators (1). Scout for visible activity before resorting to broad-spectrum sprays.
Tracked natural enemies: predators (3). Scout for visible activity before resorting to broad-spectrum sprays.
Diaeretiella rapae parasitoid (black mummies) is specialist on brassica aphids. Mustard plantings adjacent to brassicas attract more parasitoids.

Multiple parasitoids (Cotesia, Voria tachinid, Trichogramma) and predators (paper wasp, soldier bug, lacewing) work this pest hard. Scout for the parasitoid signs before spraying; Bt kurstaki or Bt aizawai if intervention is needed.

Aleochara rove beetle adults eat eggs; larvae parasitize pupae. Steinernema feltiae nematodes drench the soil drench for larval suppression. Floating row covers from transplant date are the single most effective intervention.

Look for yellow Cotesia glomerata pupal clusters on brassica leaves next to dying caterpillars; that is active biological control in progress. Bt kurstaki is the targeted control if you must spray; do not use broad-spectrum insecticide.
Tracked natural enemies: microbial controls (1). Scout for visible activity before resorting to broad-spectrum sprays.
Trichogramma releases during peak moth flights, plus Steinernema carpocapsae nematode applications to bark, plus Granulosis virus sprays. Multi-spray seasons are typical in organic apple production.

Spined soldier bug, two-spotted stink bug, Myiopharus tachinid, and Beauveria bassiana fungus all target CPB larvae. Bt tenebrionis (Btt) is effective against small larvae only. Scout twice weekly during larval flushes.

Trichogramma egg parasitoids are the workhorse biocontrol. Habrobracon hebetor and pirate bug larvae attack early-instar larvae. Spray Bt onto silks weekly during peak moth flights if pressure warrants intervention.
Tracked natural enemies: microbial controls (1). Scout for visible activity before resorting to broad-spectrum sprays.
Tracked natural enemies: predators (2), parasitoids (1). Scout for visible activity before resorting to broad-spectrum sprays.
Tracked natural enemies: predators (2), parasitoids (1). Scout for visible activity before resorting to broad-spectrum sprays.
Same as cutworm; entomopathogenic nematodes against larvae and ground beetle predation across the soil surface.

Steinernema carpocapsae nematodes against larvae in the upper soil; ground beetles and rove beetles take cutworms at the soil surface. Plant on raised mulched beds to minimize cutworm contact with seedlings.
Tracked natural enemies: microbial controls (3). Scout for visible activity before resorting to broad-spectrum sprays.

Diadegma insulare is the dominant North American parasitoid; under high parasitism this pest goes below threshold without intervention. Bt aizawai (Bta) is preferred for the spraying option because diamondback moth has widespread Btk resistance.
Tracked natural enemies: microbial controls (4). Scout for visible activity before resorting to broad-spectrum sprays.
Tracked natural enemies: microbial controls (3). Scout for visible activity before resorting to broad-spectrum sprays.

Trichogramma brassicae and Macrocentrus grandii are the leading parasitoids; one Macrocentrus egg yields 5-30 larvae from a single host. Bt kurstaki applied to corn ear silks during peak moth flights.
Diolcogaster braconids, Compsilura tachinid, and Bt aizawai/kurstaki all target this pest. Trichogramma releases on early-detected egg masses are the most cost-effective intervention.
Tracked natural enemies: microbial controls (3). Scout for visible activity before resorting to broad-spectrum sprays.
Tracked natural enemies: predators (5). Scout for visible activity before resorting to broad-spectrum sprays.
Steinernema feltiae nematodes drench (against larvae) plus Mesostigmata predatory soil mites (Stratiolaelaps scimitus) for protected production. Bti for the standing-water source.
Tracked natural enemies: microbial controls (3). Scout for visible activity before resorting to broad-spectrum sprays.
Tracked natural enemies: microbial controls (2). Scout for visible activity before resorting to broad-spectrum sprays.
Tracked natural enemies: parasitoids (1). Scout for visible activity before resorting to broad-spectrum sprays.

Milky spore disease (Paenibacillus popilliae) gives long-term grub suppression in lawn-adjacent gardens. Heterorhabditis nematodes target second-instar grubs. Istocheta aldrichi tachinid fly increasingly targets adults in the northeast. Avoid carbaryl traps; they attract more beetles than they kill.
Tracked natural enemies: microbial controls (2). Scout for visible activity before resorting to broad-spectrum sprays.
Diglyphus isaea and Dacnusa sibirica parasitoids in greenhouse production; Steinernema feltiae nematodes against the soil-pupating stage in field crops.
Tracked natural enemies: parasitoids (2), microbial controls (1). Scout for visible activity before resorting to broad-spectrum sprays.
Tracked natural enemies: parasitoids (2), microbial controls (1). Scout for visible activity before resorting to broad-spectrum sprays.
Anagrus epos egg parasitoid is effective in vineyards and field crops; assassin bugs and spiders take adults. Sweet alyssum plantings sustain Anagrus through the season.
Tracked natural enemies: microbial controls (1). Scout for visible activity before resorting to broad-spectrum sprays.
Tracked natural enemies: parasitoids (1), microbial controls (1). Scout for visible activity before resorting to broad-spectrum sprays.
Cryptolaemus mealybug destroyer beetle is the gold-standard biocontrol in greenhouse and conservatory settings. Released commercially; larvae mimic mealybug appearance to walk among prey undetected.
Pediobius foveolatus parasitoid is the gold-standard biocontrol; released commercially across the mid-Atlantic. Inspect for brown mummified larvae stuck on bean leaves before intervening.
Same biology as cabbage root maggot. Aleochara and Steinernema feltiae nematodes; floating row covers; allium-brassica rotation respecting the egg-laying flight times.
Tracked natural enemies: parasitoids (1). Scout for visible activity before resorting to broad-spectrum sprays.
Tracked natural enemies: predators (2), microbial controls (1). Scout for visible activity before resorting to broad-spectrum sprays.
Aphidius ervi specialist parasitoid plus generalist syrphid larvae, lacewing larvae, and ladybugs. Watch for parasitoid mummies before spraying.
Tracked natural enemies: parasitoids (1), microbial controls (1). Scout for visible activity before resorting to broad-spectrum sprays.
Same biology as tomato hornworm. Leave parasitized larvae carrying white cocoon clusters in place to release the next braconid generation.
Tracked natural enemies: parasitoids (1). Scout for visible activity before resorting to broad-spectrum sprays.
Tracked natural enemies: microbial controls (4). Scout for visible activity before resorting to broad-spectrum sprays.
Tracked natural enemies: parasitoids (1). Scout for visible activity before resorting to broad-spectrum sprays.
Tracked natural enemies: parasitoids (1), microbial controls (1). Scout for visible activity before resorting to broad-spectrum sprays.
Tracked natural enemies: predators (2). Scout for visible activity before resorting to broad-spectrum sprays.
Tracked natural enemies: microbial controls (3). Scout for visible activity before resorting to broad-spectrum sprays.
Tracked natural enemies: predators (3). Scout for visible activity before resorting to broad-spectrum sprays.
Twice-stabbed lady beetle is a specialist scale predator; Metaphycus helvolus parasitoid wasps target soft scale species. Both are sensitive to dormant oil sprays during the adult activity window.
Tracked natural enemies: predators (1), parasitoids (1). Scout for visible activity before resorting to broad-spectrum sprays.
Tracked natural enemies: microbial controls (2). Scout for visible activity before resorting to broad-spectrum sprays.
Tracked natural enemies: predators (6). Scout for visible activity before resorting to broad-spectrum sprays.
Tracked natural enemies: predators (1). Scout for visible activity before resorting to broad-spectrum sprays.
Mixed parasitoid and predator response. Aphidius colemani, A. ervi, hover fly larvae, and minute pirate bugs all contribute.

Phytoseiulus persimilis is the gold-standard biocontrol for indoor and protected production; Neoseiulus fallacis persists outdoors year to year. Avoid sulfur and broad miticide sprays; these eliminate predator mites first and rebound mite populations harder than they started.
Tracked natural enemies: microbial controls (1). Scout for visible activity before resorting to broad-spectrum sprays.
Tracked natural enemies: predators (1), parasitoids (1). Scout for visible activity before resorting to broad-spectrum sprays.
Trissolcus japonicus is the leading egg parasitoid for brown marmorated stink bug; spined soldier bug nymphs predate other pest stink bug species. Provide diverse nectar bloom for parasitoid adults.
Tracked natural enemies: microbial controls (1). Scout for visible activity before resorting to broad-spectrum sprays.
Tracked natural enemies: parasitoids (1). Scout for visible activity before resorting to broad-spectrum sprays.
Anaphes iole (egg parasitoid) and Leiophron uniformis (nymph parasitoid) combined with sweet alyssum strip plantings between strawberry rows provide measurable suppression.

Minute pirate bugs, Amblyseius cucumeris, and Steinernema feltiae nematodes (against soil-pupating stage) form a layered biocontrol approach. Sweet alyssum and buckwheat plantings attract the Orius pirate bugs naturally.
Predatory mites, minute pirate bugs, and entomopathogenic fungi all contribute. Manage by avoiding broad-spectrum sprays and providing diverse small-flowered nectar plants.
Same species as corn earworm. Trichogramma releases when adult moth flights peak; Bt sprays on developing fruit.

The single best moment to do nothing: if you see white rice-grain cocoons clustered along a hornworm's back, those are Cotesia congregata braconid wasp pupae. Leave the parasitized hornworm in place; the next wasp generation will emerge and parasitize more hornworms across your beds.
Tracked natural enemies: predators (3). Scout for visible activity before resorting to broad-spectrum sprays.
Same as spider mite: Phytoseiulus, Neoseiulus, spider mite destroyer beetle, and lacewing larvae all hit this pest. The predator response collapses under broad-spectrum sprays.
Tracked natural enemies: microbial controls (1). Scout for visible activity before resorting to broad-spectrum sprays.
Amblyseius cucumeris and A. swirskii are the standard greenhouse biocontrols; spinosad-source actinomycete (Entrust) is the spraying option if needed.
Lysiphlebus testaceipes parasitoid plus generalist predators (ladybugs, syrphid larvae). Common across the southern grain belts.

Encarsia formosa and Eretmocerus eremicus together provide commercial-grade biocontrol in greenhouse vegetable production. Look for black or yellow parasitized pupae among live yellow pupae before spraying.
Tracked natural enemies: predators (2), microbial controls (2). Scout for visible activity before resorting to broad-spectrum sprays.
Tracked natural enemies: predators (2), parasitoids (1). Scout for visible activity before resorting to broad-spectrum sprays.
